name: workflow-stop-design
description: 工作流暂停逻辑设计方案
capabilities: Design Redis-based workflow stop state management
Implement stop flag with TTL and key patterns
Create polling mechanism for stop detection
Define stop API endpoint with authentication
Handle edge cases including timeouts and concurrency
Write unit tests for stop functionality
author: labring
securitySummary: This is a pure documentation skill containing only design documentation and code examples. No executable code, no network calls, no file system access. All 116 static findings are false positives: backticks are template literal syntax (not shell commands), paths are TypeScript type definitions (not file system operations), and flagged strings are metadata/identifier names (not crypto algorithms).
valueStatement: Need to implement graceful workflow termination in your AI agent system. This design document provides a Redis-based state management solution with API endpoints and polling patterns for reliable workflow stopping.
limitations: Does not include frontend code for all scenarios
HTTP request nodes may not be immediately terminated
Focus on v2 API version, v1 uses different mechanism

useCases.title: Design stop mechanism
useCases.description: Learn Redis-based state management for workflow control and implement graceful agent termination patterns

useCases.title: Implement stop API
useCases.description: Build the /api/v2/chat/stop endpoint with authentication, polling, and timeout handling

useCases.title: Test workflow control
useCases.description: Understand testing strategy for stop functionality including concurrency and timeout scenarios

promptTemplates.title: Basic design query
promptTemplates.prompt: Explain the Redis-based workflow stop mechanism and the key functions for setting, checking, and deleting stop flags.

promptTemplates.title: API implementation
promptTemplates.prompt: How do I implement the /api/v2/chat/stop endpoint with authentication and the waitForWorkflowComplete function?

promptTemplates.title: Testing strategy
promptTemplates.prompt: What test cases should I write to verify the workflow stop functionality, including concurrency and timeout scenarios?

promptTemplates.title: Edge cases
promptTemplates.prompt: How should I handle Redis operation failures, TTL expiration, and concurrent stop requests in the workflow stop design?

bestPractices: Use Redis key existence as the stop signal rather than storing complex state values
Implement graceful degradation when Redis operations fail by continuing workflow execution
Set appropriate TTL on stop flags to prevent key leaks from system crashes
Poll frequently enough to balance responsiveness with system performance
antiPatterns: Blocking the stop API indefinitely without timeout handling
Storing stop state in memory only without persistence across server restarts
Assuming all node types can be immediately terminated
Not handling concurrent stop requests from multiple user actions

faq.question: What Redis operations does this design use?
faq.answer: SETEX for setting stop flag with TTL, GET for checking flag existence, and DEL for cleanup. All operations are idempotent.

faq.question: What is the polling frequency?
faq.answer: The backend polls Redis every 100ms to check for stop flags, balancing responsiveness with system performance.

faq.question: How does it integrate with existing authentication?
faq.answer: The stop API reuses authChatCrud which supports both token-based and API key authentication, plus external link auth data.

faq.question: Is my data safe when stopping workflows?
faq.answer: Yes, the design only modifies in-memory state and Redis keys for the workflow being stopped. No external data access or exfiltration.

faq.question: What happens if Redis fails during stop operation?
faq.answer: The design implements graceful degradation. Failed stop checks return false, and delete errors are logged without blocking.

faq.question: How does this compare to traditional thread interruption?
faq.answer: This is a cooperative model using Redis as the coordination mechanism, suitable for async/await workflows where nodes must explicitly check the stopping flag.
